summary: Julia Evans shares practical strategies for participating in meetings as a remote worker. She identifies two key scenarios: all-remote meetings (which work fine) and mixed meetings with mostly local participants (which are much harder for the remote person). The core problem is that remote participants can't easily interrupt or signal they want to speak. She describes learning to be deliberately loud and explicit about wanting to talk, and also notes that local participants can actively help by soliciting input from remote attendees.
key_points: All-remote meetings work well because everyone faces the same communication constraints, but mixed meetings with one remote person are a disaster
The fundamental problem for remote participants is the inability to visually signal a desire to speak, making natural turn-taking nearly impossible
Explicit, slightly awkward communication protocols (announcing when you want to speak and when you're done) are effective workarounds
Local participants bear responsibility too — actively asking remote people for input reveals they often have valuable things to say but face too high a barrier to volunteer
Learning to interrupt effectively as a remote participant is a skill that takes significant time to develop
money_quotes: Scenario #2 is a disaster. It took me a long time to understand why, but I think the primary reason is that it's very hard to interrupt and indicate that I want to speak when I'm remote.
if I just wait for a break in the conversation to say something… well, it will never happen :)
Now if I have something to say I just say "HI I HAVE A THING TO SAY". And people say "yes julia?" and then I say the thing. It feels awkward but it works for me.
So my interactions are awkwardly delimited by HI I HAVE A THING TO SAY – I AM SAYING THE THING - I AM DONE SPEAKING NOW YOU MAY PROCEED. This is great and it works.
Normally, this means that the local people do all the talking and the remotes are totally silent (because the barrier to entry is high).
they frequently did have something really interesting to say! They were just not saying it.
key_insight: Mixed local-remote meetings systematically silence remote participants, and the fix requires both remote people learning to be deliberately explicit about turn-taking and local people actively soliciting remote input.
